title: Nuno felting on velvet
subtitle: Bonding merino roving onto cotton or silk velvet for a textured, gathered surface
excerpt: Lay fine merino roving over the pile side of cotton or silk velvet, then wet-felt the two together. The shrinking wool pulls the velvet into a deeply textured, crinkled surface unlike any other nuno-felting ground fabric.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from contemporary nuno-felting practice. Velvet as a nuno-felting ground fabric is documented in current British feltmaking texts and taught at specialist workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A technique in which wool fibres are bonded into an open-weave fabric base using wet-felting agitation. The fibres pass through the open weave and lock around the fabric threads, bonding permanently.
The stage of wet felting where vigorous manipulation shrinks the piece to the desired size and tightens the fibre structure.

content.text: Velvet is one of the most dramatic grounds for
because its long pile captures and holds the wool fibres well, and the shrinkage from

gathers the velvet into a dense, sculptural texture. Both cotton velvet and silk velvet work well. The wool layer needs to be thinner than for silk chiffon to avoid making the finished piece too heavy.

attrs.title: Velvet pile direction
attrs.body: Lay wool on the pile side only. The pile must face upward so the fibres penetrate through to the ground weave.

items.name: Cotton or silk velvet, pile side up
Fine merino roving (21 micron or finer)
items.qty: roughly 20 g per 30 x 30 cm of velvet

content.text: Lay the velvet on the bamboo rolling mat, pile side up. Smooth it flat. Pre-wet the velvet with cool water before laying the roving; this helps the wool fibres penetrate the pile more easily.

Tease fine merino into very thin wisps and lay one thin, even layer over the velvet, covering the full surface. The layer should be thin enough that the velvet colour shows through. Add a second layer at 90 degrees to the first if a heavier surface texture is desired.

Cover the roving with a piece of netting or organza to protect the surface. Wet thoroughly with hot soapy water, pressing flat with a sponge. Begin rubbing with a flat palm in circular motions through the net. After 5 minutes, lift the net and check that the wool has begun to grip the velvet. Remove the net and continue rubbing directly.

Roll the velvet and wool together on the bamboo mat and roll back and forth 50 times. Unroll, rotate 90 degrees, and roll again. The velvet will begin to gather and crinkle as the wool shrinks. Continue rolling until the desired texture has formed and the wool is firmly bonded.
Rinse in warm then cool water until the water runs clear. Press out excess water in a towel. Lay flat or hang to dry. The texture will be slightly more pronounced once fully dry.

items.problem: The wool peels away from the velvet rather than bonding
items.cause: The velvet was not pre-wetted or the initial rubbing phase was too short
items.fix: Pre-wet the velvet before laying the roving. The first rubbing phase must be at least 8 to 10 minutes for the fibres to penetrate the pile and reach the ground weave.

items.problem: The velvet surface is flat rather than crinkled
items.cause: The roving was too thick or the rolling phase was too short
items.fix: Thin the roving further for the next piece. The crinkle develops as the wool shrinks; if fulling stopped too early, wet the piece again and continue rolling.
